title: The Initialize Method 🏗️
description: Automatically set up your objects the moment they are born.

slides.title: The Constructor 🏗️
slides.content:
It's tedious to create an object with `.new`, and then separately call a `set_name` method to give it data.

Wouldn't it be great if you could pass the name directly into the `.new` method the exact moment the object is created?

You can, using the `initialize` method!

slides.title: Automated Setup
slides.content:
When you call `.new` on a Class, Ruby automatically looks inside the Class blueprint for a method named exactly `initialize`.

If it finds it, Ruby executes it instantly, using whatever arguments you passed into `.new`.

```ruby
class Dog
  def initialize(dog_name)
    @name = dog_name
  end
end
```

slides.title: Initialize in Action
slides.content: Run this! Notice how we pass `"Fido"` straight into the `.new` method, and it flows directly into the `initialize` parameters.
slides.codeSnippet:
class Dog
  def initialize(breed)
    @breed = breed
    puts "A new #{@breed} was just born!"
  end
end

# The string is handed directly to initialize
my_dog = Dog.new("Golden Retriever")

quizData.question: Which method is automatically triggered by Ruby the exact moment you call `.new` on a class?
quizData.explanation: If a method is named exactly `initialize`, Ruby knows it contains the startup sequence logic for the object.

slides.title: Guaranteed State 🔒
slides.content:
Using `initialize` guarantees that your objects are birthed correctly.

If your class defines `def initialize(name, age)`, and a developer tries to run `Dog.new` with no arguments, Ruby will instantly crash and throw an error!

This prevents the creation of empty, buggy data.

quizData.question: If your `initialize` method asks for a parameter `(name)`, what happens if you try to build the object with `Dog.new()`?

options.text: Ruby skips the initialize method entirely.

quizData.explanation: Because `.new` feeds its arguments straight into `initialize`, the exact same parameter rules apply!
